pages.title: Foreword
pages.html:
Foreword

Each of the written-up exposés gives the substance of several consecutive oral exposés. It has not seemed useful to specify their dates.

Exposé VII, to which reference is made on various occasions in the course of Exposé VIII, was not written up by the lecturer, who in the oral lectures had confined himself to sketching the language of descent in general categories, taking a strictly utilitarian point of view and without entering into the logical difficulties raised by this language. It appeared that a correct exposé of this language would exceed the limits of the present notes, if only by its length. For a formal account of the theory of descent, I refer to an article in preparation by Jean Giraud. Pending its appearance1, I think that an attentive reader will have no difficulty in supplying by his own means the phantom references of Exposé VIII.

Other oral exposés, coming after Exposé XI, and alluded to at certain places in the text, were likewise not written up, and were intended to form the substance of an Exposé XII and an Exposé XIII. The first of these oral exposés took up again, within the framework of schemes and analytic spaces with nilpotent elements (as they are introduced in the Séminaire Cartan 1960/61), the construction of the analytic space associated to a prescheme locally of finite type over a complete valued field \(k\), the theorems of GAGA type in the case where \(k\) is the field of complex numbers, and the application to the comparison of the fundamental group defined by transcendental means and the fundamental group studied in these notes (compare A. Grothendieck, Fondements de la Géométrie Algébrique, Séminaire Bourbaki 190, page 10, December 1959). The last oral exposés sketched the generalization of the methods developed in the text for the study of coverings admitting tame ramification, and of the structure of the fundamental group of a complete curve deprived of a finite number of points (compare loc. cit. , 182, page 27, theorem 14). These exposés introduce no essentially new idea, which is why it has not seemed indispensable to give a formal write-up of them before the appearance of the corresponding chapters of the Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique2.

On the other hand, the Lefschetz-type theorems for the fundamental group and the Picard group, both from the local and the global point of view, were the object of a separate Seminar in 1962, which has been completely written up and is at the disposal of users3. Let us point out that the results developed both in the present Seminar and in that of 1962 will be used in an essential way in the appearance of several key results in the étale cohomology of preschemes, which will be the object of a Seminar (conducted by M. Artin and myself) in 1963/64, currently in preparation4.

Exposés I to IV, of an essentially local and very elementary nature, will be entirely absorbed into chapter IV of the Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique, whose first part is at the press and which will no doubt be published around the end of 64. They may nevertheless be useful to a reader who might wish to become acquainted with the essential properties of smooth, étale, or flat morphisms, before entering into the mysteries of a systematic treatise. As for the other exposés, they will be absorbed into chapter VIII5 of the “Éléments”, whose publication can scarcely be envisaged before several years.

Bures, June 1963.

footnotes.html: Now published: J. Giraud, Méthodes de la descente, Mémoire 2 of the Société mathématique de France, 1964.
They are included in the present volume in Exp. XII by Mme Raynaud with a proof different from the original proof presented in the oral Seminar (cf. introduction).
Cohomologie étale des faisceaux cohérents et théorèmes de Lefschetz locaux et globaux (SGA 2), published by North Holland Pub. Cie.
Cohomologie étale des schémas (cited SGA 4), to appear in this same series.

In fact, as a consequence of a modification of the plan initially envisaged for the Éléments, the study of the fundamental group is there postponed to a chapter later than the one just indicated. Compare the introduction preceding the present “Foreword”.
